name: The Dhammapada
description: The Dhammapada ('Path of Dhamma'), a collection of 423 verses attributed to the Buddha, is one of the most widely read Buddhist scriptures. Level 1 items contain individual verses from the F. Max Muller translation (1881). Level 2 items are the 26 traditional chapters with thematic summaries.

attribution.note: English translation by F. Max Muller from The Sacred Books of the East, Vol. X, Part I (1881). Text from Project Gutenberg eBook #2017.

items.name: Verse 1: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is...

sections.Verse: 1. All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.

items.name: Verse 2: All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is...

sections.Verse: 2. All that we are is the result of what we have thought: it is founded on our thoughts, it is made up of our thoughts. If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.

items.name: Verse 3: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"--in...

sections.Verse: 3. "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"--in those who harbour such thoughts hatred will never cease.

items.name: Verse 4: "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"--in...

sections.Verse: 4. "He abused me, he beat me, he defeated me, he robbed me,"--in those who do not harbour such thoughts hatred will cease.

items.name: Verse 5: For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases...

sections.Verse: 5. For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time: hatred ceases by love, this is an old rule.

items.name: Verse 6: The world does not know that we must all come to an end...

sections.Verse: 6. The world does not know that we must all come to an end here;--but those who know it, their quarrels cease at once.

items.name: Verse 7: He who lives looking for pleasures only, his senses...

sections.Verse: 7. He who lives looking for pleasures only, his senses uncontrolled, immoderate in his food, idle, and weak, Mara (the tempter) will certainly overthrow him, as the wind throws down a weak tree.

items.name: Verse 8: He who lives without looking for pleasures, his senses well...

sections.Verse: 8. He who lives without looking for pleasures, his senses well controlled, moderate in his food, faithful and strong, him Mara will certainly not overthrow, any more than the wind throws down a rocky mountain.

items.name: Verse 9: He who wishes to put on the yellow dress without having cleansed...

sections.Verse: 9. He who wishes to put on the yellow dress without having cleansed himself from sin, who disregards temperance and truth, is unworthy of the yellow dress.

items.name: Verse 10: But he who has cleansed himself from sin, is well grounded in...

sections.Verse: 10. But he who has cleansed himself from sin, is well grounded in all virtues, and regards also temperance and truth, he is indeed worthy of the yellow dress.

items.name: Verse 11: They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth,...

sections.Verse: 11. They who imagine truth in untruth, and see untruth in truth, never arrive at truth, but follow vain desires.

items.name: Verse 12: They who know truth in truth, and untruth in untruth, arrive at...

sections.Verse: 12. They who know truth in truth, and untruth in untruth, arrive at truth, and follow true desires.

items.name: Verse 13: As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break...

sections.Verse: 13. As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break through an unreflecting mind.

items.name: Verse 14: As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion...

sections.Verse: 14. As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion will not break through a well-reflecting mind.

items.name: Verse 15: The evil-doer mourns in this world, and he mourns in the next;...

sections.Verse: 15. The evil-doer mourns in this world, and he mourns in the next; he mourns in both. He mourns and suffers when he sees the evil of his own work.

items.name: Verse 16: The virtuous man delights in this world, and he delights in the...

sections.Verse: 16. The virtuous man delights in this world, and he delights in the next; he delights in both. He delights and rejoices, when he sees the purity of his own work.

items.name: Verse 17: The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next;...

sections.Verse: 17. The evil-doer suffers in this world, and he suffers in the next; he suffers in both. He suffers when he thinks of the evil he has done; he suffers more when going on the evil path.

items.name: Verse 18: The virtuous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the...

sections.Verse: 18. The virtuous man is happy in this world, and he is happy in the next; he is happy in both. He is happy when he thinks of the good he has done; he is still more happy when going on the good path.

items.name: Verse 19: The thoughtless man, even if he can recite a large portion (of...

sections.Verse: 19. The thoughtless man, even if he can recite a large portion (of the law), but is not a doer of it, has no share in the priesthood, but is like a cowherd counting the cows of others.

items.name: Verse 20: The follower of the law, even if he can recite only a small...

sections.Verse: 20. The follower of the law, even if he can recite only a small portion (of the law), but, having forsaken passion and hatred and foolishness, possesses true knowledge and serenity of mind, he, caring for nothing in this world or that to come, has indeed a share in the priesthood.

items.name: Chapter I: The Twin-Verses

sections.Brief: The opening verses establish the fundamental Buddhist teaching: mind is the forerunner of all actions. Paired verses contrast the outcomes of evil and pure thought, hatred and love, heedlessness and awareness.

items.name: Verse 21: Earnestness is the path of immortality (Nirvana),...

sections.Verse: 21. Earnestness is the path of immortality (Nirvana), thoughtlessness the path of death. Those who are in earnest do not die, those who are thoughtless are as if dead already.

items.name: Verse 22: Those who are advanced in earnestness, having understood this...

sections.Verse: 22. Those who are advanced in earnestness, having understood this clearly, delight in earnestness, and rejoice in the knowledge of the Ariyas (the elect).

items.name: Verse 23: These wise people, meditative, steady, always possessed of...

sections.Verse: 23. These wise people, meditative, steady, always possessed of strong powers, attain to Nirvana, the highest happiness.

items.name: Verse 24: If an earnest person has roused himself, if he is not forgetful,...

sections.Verse: 24. If an earnest person has roused himself, if he is not forgetful, if his deeds are pure, if he acts with consideration, if he restrains himself, and lives according to law,--then his glory will increase.

items.name: Verse 25: By rousing himself, by earnestness, by restraint and control,...

sections.Verse: 25. By rousing himself, by earnestness, by restraint and control, the wise man may make for himself an island which no flood can overwhelm.

items.name: Verse 26: Fools follow after vanity, men of evil wisdom. The wise man...

sections.Verse: 26. Fools follow after vanity, men of evil wisdom. The wise man keeps earnestness as his best jewel.

items.name: Verse 27: Follow not after vanity, nor after the enjoyment of love and...

sections.Verse: 27. Follow not after vanity, nor after the enjoyment of love and lust! He who is earnest and meditative, obtains ample joy.

items.name: Verse 28: When the learned man drives away vanity by earnestness, he, the...

sections.Verse: 28. When the learned man drives away vanity by earnestness, he, the wise, climbing the terraced heights of wisdom, looks down upon the fools, serene he looks upon the toiling crowd, as one that stands on a mountain looks down upon them that stand upon the plain.

items.name: Verse 29: Earnest among the thoughtless, awake among the sleepers, the...

sections.Verse: 29. Earnest among the thoughtless, awake among the sleepers, the wise man advances like a racer, leaving behind the hack.

items.name: Verse 30: By earnestness did Maghavan (Indra) rise to the lordship of the...

sections.Verse: 30. By earnestness did Maghavan (Indra) rise to the lordship of the gods. People praise earnestness; thoughtlessness is always blamed.

items.name: Verse 31: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in earnestness, who looks...

sections.Verse: 31. A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in earnestness, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, moves about like fire, burning all his fetters, small or large.

items.name: Verse 32: A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in reflection, who looks with...

sections.Verse: 32. A Bhikshu (mendicant) who delights in reflection, who looks with fear on thoughtlessness, cannot fall away (from his perfect state)--he is close upon Nirvana.

items.name: Chapter II: On Earnestness

sections.Brief: Earnestness (appamada) is the path to the deathless; heedlessness is the path to death. The wise who meditate earnestly attain Nirvana, while the thoughtless remain bound.

items.name: Verse 33: As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes...

sections.Verse: 33. As a fletcher makes straight his arrow, a wise man makes straight his trembling and unsteady thought, which is difficult to guard, difficult to hold back.

items.name: Verse 34: As a fish taken from his watery home and thrown on dry ground,...

sections.Verse: 34. As a fish taken from his watery home and thrown on dry ground, our thought trembles all over in order to escape the dominion of Mara (the tempter).

items.name: Verse 35: It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and...

sections.Verse: 35. It is good to tame the mind, which is difficult to hold in and flighty, rushing wherever it listeth; a tamed mind brings happiness.

items.name: Verse 36: Let the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are difficult to...

sections.Verse: 36. Let the wise man guard his thoughts, for they are difficult to perceive, very artful, and they rush wherever they list: thoughts well guarded bring happiness.

items.name: Verse 37: Those who bridle their mind which travels far, moves about...

sections.Verse: 37. Those who bridle their mind which travels far, moves about alone, is without a body, and hides in the chamber (of the heart), will be free from the bonds of Mara (the tempter).

items.name: Verse 38: If a man's thoughts are unsteady, if he does not know the true...

sections.Verse: 38. If a man's thoughts are unsteady, if he does not know the true law, if his peace of mind is troubled, his knowledge will never be perfect.

items.name: Verse 39: If a man's thoughts are not dissipated, if his mind is not...

sections.Verse: 39. If a man's thoughts are not dissipated, if his mind is not perplexed, if he has ceased to think of good or evil, then there is no fear for him while he is watchful.

items.name: Verse 40: Knowing that this body is (fragile) like a jar, and making this...

sections.Verse: 40. Knowing that this body is (fragile) like a jar, and making this thought firm like a fortress, one should attack Mara (the tempter) with the weapon of knowledge, one should watch him when conquered, and should never rest.

items.name: Verse 41: Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised,...

sections.Verse: 41. Before long, alas! this body will lie on the earth, despised, without understanding, like a useless log.

items.name: Verse 42: Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a...

sections.Verse: 42. Whatever a hater may do to a hater, or an enemy to an enemy, a wrongly-directed mind will do us greater mischief.

items.name: Verse 43: Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other...

sections.Verse: 43. Not a mother, not a father will do so much, nor any other relative; a well-directed mind will do us greater service.

items.name: Chapter III: Thought

sections.Brief: The flickering, fickle mind must be straightened and guarded like a fletcher straightens an arrow. A tamed mind brings happiness; an uncontrolled mind brings suffering.

items.name: Verse 44: Who shall overcome this earth, and the world of Yama (the lord...

sections.Verse: 44. Who shall overcome this earth, and the world of Yama (the lord of the departed), and the world of the gods? Who shall find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower?

items.name: Verse 45: The disciple will overcome the earth, and the world of Yama, and...

sections.Verse: 45. The disciple will overcome the earth, and the world of Yama, and the world of the gods. The disciple will find out the plainly shown path of virtue, as a clever man finds out the (right) flower.

items.name: Verse 46: He who knows that this body is like froth, and has learnt that...

sections.Verse: 46. He who knows that this body is like froth, and has learnt that it is as unsubstantial as a mirage, will break the flower-pointed arrow of Mara, and never see the king of death.

items.name: Verse 47: Death carries off a man who is gathering flowers and whose mind...

sections.Verse: 47. Death carries off a man who is gathering flowers and whose mind is distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.

items.name: Verse 48: Death subdues a man who is gathering flowers, and whose mind is...

sections.Verse: 48. Death subdues a man who is gathering flowers, and whose mind is distracted, before he is satiated in his pleasures.

items.name: Verse 49: As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the...

sections.Verse: 49. As the bee collects nectar and departs without injuring the flower, or its colour or scent, so let a sage dwell in his village.

items.name: Verse 50: Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or...

sections.Verse: 50. Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or omission, but his own misdeeds and negligences should a sage take notice of.

items.name: Verse 51: Like a beautiful flower, full of colour, but without scent, are...

sections.Verse: 51. Like a beautiful flower, full of colour, but without scent, are the fine but fruitless words of him who does not act accordingly.

items.name: Verse 52: But, like a beautiful flower, full of colour and full of scent,...

sections.Verse: 52. But, like a beautiful flower, full of colour and full of scent, are the fine and fruitful words of him who acts accordingly.

items.name: Verse 53: As many kinds of wreaths can be made from a heap of flowers, so...

sections.Verse: 53. As many kinds of wreaths can be made from a heap of flowers, so many good things may be achieved by a mortal when once he is born.

items.name: Verse 54: The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor (that...

sections.Verse: 54. The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, nor (that of) sandal-wood, or of Tagara and Mallika flowers; but the odour of good people travels even against the wind; a good man pervades every place.

items.name: Verse 55: Sandal-wood or Tagara, a lotus-flower, or a Vassiki, among these...

sections.Verse: 55. Sandal-wood or Tagara, a lotus-flower, or a Vassiki, among these sorts of perfumes, the perfume of virtue is unsurpassed.

items.name: Verse 56: Mean is the scent that comes from Tagara and sandal-wood;--the...

sections.Verse: 56. Mean is the scent that comes from Tagara and sandal-wood;--the perfume of those who possess virtue rises up to the gods as the highest.

items.name: Verse 57: Of the people who possess these virtues, who live without...

sections.Verse: 57. Of the people who possess these virtues, who live without thoughtlessness, and who are emancipated through true knowledge, Mara, the tempter, never finds the way. 58, 59. As on a heap of rubbish cast upon the highway the lily will grow full of sweet perfume and delight, thus the disciple of the truly enlightened Buddha shines forth by his knowledge among those who are like rubbish, among the people that walk in darkness.

items.name: Chapter IV: Flowers

sections.Brief: Life is as fleeting as a flower. The wise gather virtue as a bee gathers honey without harming the blossom. Death sweeps away the heedless like a flood carries a sleeping village.

items.name: Verse 60: Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who...

sections.Verse: 60. Long is the night to him who is awake; long is a mile to him who is tired; long is life to the foolish who do not know the true law.

items.name: Verse 61: If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his...

sections.Verse: 61. If a traveller does not meet with one who is his better, or his equal, let him firmly keep to his solitary journey; there is no companionship with a fool.

items.name: Verse 62: "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me," with...

sections.Verse: 62. "These sons belong to me, and this wealth belongs to me," with such thoughts a fool is tormented. He himself does not belong to himself; how much less sons and wealth?

items.name: Verse 63: The fool who knows his foolishness, is wise at least so far. But...

sections.Verse: 63. The fool who knows his foolishness, is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.

items.name: Verse 64: If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he...

sections.Verse: 64. If a fool be associated with a wise man even all his life, he will perceive the truth as little as a spoon perceives the taste of soup.

items.name: Verse 65: If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a...

sections.Verse: 65. If an intelligent man be associated for one minute only with a wise man, he will soon perceive the truth, as the tongue perceives the taste of soup.

items.name: Verse 66: Fools of little understanding have themselves for their greatest...

sections.Verse: 66. Fools of little understanding have themselves for their greatest enemies, for they do evil deeds which must bear bitter fruits.

items.name: Verse 67: That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the...

sections.Verse: 67. That deed is not well done of which a man must repent, and the reward of which he receives crying and with a tearful face.

items.name: Verse 68: No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and...

sections.Verse: 68. No, that deed is well done of which a man does not repent, and the reward of which he receives gladly and cheerfully.

items.name: Verse 69: As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool...

sections.Verse: 69. As long as the evil deed done does not bear fruit, the fool thinks it is like honey; but when it ripens, then the fool suffers grief.

items.name: Verse 70: Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with...

sections.Verse: 70. Let a fool month after month eat his food (like an ascetic) with the tip of a blade of Kusa grass, yet he is not worth the sixteenth particle of those who have well weighed the law.

items.name: Verse 71: An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn (suddenly);...

sections.Verse: 71. An evil deed, like newly-drawn milk, does not turn (suddenly); smouldering, like fire covered by ashes, it follows the fool.

items.name: Verse 72: And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow...

sections.Verse: 72. And when the evil deed, after it has become known, brings sorrow to the fool, then it destroys his bright lot, nay, it cleaves his head.

items.name: Verse 73: Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among...

sections.Verse: 73. Let the fool wish for a false reputation, for precedence among the Bhikshus, for lordship in the convents, for worship among other people!

items.name: Verse 74: "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that...

sections.Verse: 74. "May both the layman and he who has left the world think that this is done by me; may they be subject to me in everything which is to be done or is not to be done," thus is the mind of the fool, and his desire and pride increase.

items.name: Verse 75: "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that...

sections.Verse: 75. "One is the road that leads to wealth, another the road that leads to Nirvana;" if the Bhikshu, the disciple of Buddha, has learnt this, he will not yearn for honour, he will strive after separation from the world.

items.name: Chapter V: The Fool

sections.Brief: The fool who knows his foolishness is wise to that extent. The company of fools brings long-lasting pain; the company of the wise brings joy like meeting kinsmen.

items.name: Verse 76: If you see an intelligent man who tells you where true treasures...

sections.Verse: 76. If you see an intelligent man who tells you where true treasures are to be found, who shows what is to be avoided, and administers reproofs, follow that wise man; it will be better, not worse, for those who follow him.

items.name: Verse 77: Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is...

sections.Verse: 77. Let him admonish, let him teach, let him forbid what is improper!--he will be beloved of the good, by the bad he will be hated.

items.name: Verse 78: Do not have evil-doers for friends, do not have low people for...

sections.Verse: 78. Do not have evil-doers for friends, do not have low people for friends: have virtuous people for friends, have for friends the best of men.

items.name: Verse 79: He who drinks in the law lives happily with a serene mind: the...

sections.Verse: 79. He who drinks in the law lives happily with a serene mind: the sage rejoices always in the law, as preached by the elect (Ariyas).

items.name: Verse 80: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend...

sections.Verse: 80. Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; wise people fashion themselves.

items.name: Verse 81: As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter...

sections.Verse: 81. As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter not amidst blame and praise.

items.name: Verse 82: Wise people, after they have listened to the laws, become...

sections.Verse: 82. Wise people, after they have listened to the laws, become serene, like a deep, smooth, and still lake.

items.name: Verse 83: Good people walk on whatever befall, the good do not prattle,...

sections.Verse: 83. Good people walk on whatever befall, the good do not prattle, longing for pleasure; whether touched by happiness or sorrow wise people never appear elated or depressed.

items.name: Verse 84: If, whether for his own sake, or for the sake of others, a man...

sections.Verse: 84. If, whether for his own sake, or for the sake of others, a man wishes neither for a son, nor for wealth, nor for lordship, and if he does not wish for his own success by unfair means, then he is good, wise, and virtuous.

items.name: Verse 85: Few are there among men who arrive at the other shore (become...

sections.Verse: 85. Few are there among men who arrive at the other shore (become Arhats); the other people here run up and down the shore.

items.name: Verse 86: But those who, when the law has been well preached to them,...

sections.Verse: 86. But those who, when the law has been well preached to them, follow the law, will pass across the dominion of death, however difficult to overcome. 87, 88. A wise man should leave the dark state (of ordinary life), and follow the bright state (of the Bhikshu). After going from his home to a homeless state, he should in his retirement look for enjoyment where there seemed to be no enjoyment. Leaving all pleasures behind, and calling nothing his own, the wise man should purge himself from all the troubles of the mind.

items.name: Verse 89: Those whose mind is well grounded in the (seven) elements of...

sections.Verse: 89. Those whose mind is well grounded in the (seven) elements of knowledge, who without clinging to anything, rejoice in freedom from attachment, whose appetites have been conquered, and who are full of light, are free (even) in this world.

items.name: Chapter VI: The Wise Man (Pandita)

sections.Brief: The wise man who points out faults is to be followed like a guide to hidden treasure. The wise are disciplined in body, speech, and mind. Like a deep lake, calm and clear, the wise become serene.

items.name: Verse 90: There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and...

sections.Verse: 90. There is no suffering for him who has finished his journey, and abandoned grief, who has freed himself on all sides, and thrown off all fetters.

items.name: Verse 91: They depart with their thoughts well-collected, they are not...

sections.Verse: 91. They depart with their thoughts well-collected, they are not happy in their abode; like swans who have left their lake, they leave their house and home.

items.name: Verse 92: Men who have no riches, who live on recognised food, who have...

sections.Verse: 92. Men who have no riches, who live on recognised food, who have perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), their path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.

items.name: Verse 93: He whose appetites are stilled, who is not absorbed in...

sections.Verse: 93. He whose appetites are stilled, who is not absorbed in enjoyment, who has perceived void and unconditioned freedom (Nirvana), his path is difficult to understand, like that of birds in the air.

items.name: Verse 94: The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in...

sections.Verse: 94. The gods even envy him whose senses, like horses well broken in by the driver, have been subdued, who is free from pride, and free from appetites.

items.name: Verse 95: Such a one who does his duty is tolerant like the earth, like...

sections.Verse: 95. Such a one who does his duty is tolerant like the earth, like Indra's bolt; he is like a lake without mud; no new births are in store for him.

items.name: Verse 96: His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has...

sections.Verse: 96. His thought is quiet, quiet are his word and deed, when he has obtained freedom by true knowledge, when he has thus become a quiet man.

items.name: Verse 97: The man who is free from credulity, but knows the uncreated, who...

sections.Verse: 97. The man who is free from credulity, but knows the uncreated, who has cut all ties, removed all temptations, renounced all desires, he is the greatest of men.

items.name: Verse 98: In a hamlet or in a forest, in the deep water or on the dry...

sections.Verse: 98. In a hamlet or in a forest, in the deep water or on the dry land, wherever venerable persons (Arhanta) dwell, that place is delightful.

items.name: Verse 99: Forests are delightful; where the world finds no delight, there...

sections.Verse: 99. Forests are delightful; where the world finds no delight, there the passionless will find delight, for they look not for pleasures.

items.name: Chapter VII: The Venerable (Arhat)

sections.Brief: The Arhat (venerable one) has completed the journey, is free from sorrow, and is liberated in all things. The mindful exert themselves; like swans leaving a lake, they leave their earthly attachments behind.

items.name: Verse 100: Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of...

sections.Verse: 100. Even though a speech be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of sense is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

items.name: Verse 101: Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up...

sections.Verse: 101. Even though a Gatha (poem) be a thousand (of words), but made up of senseless words, one word of a Gatha is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

items.name: Verse 102: Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words,...

sections.Verse: 102. Though a man recite a hundred Gathas made up of senseless words, one word of the law is better, which if a man hears, he becomes quiet.

items.name: Verse 103: If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and...

sections.Verse: 103. If one man conquer in battle a thousand times thousand men, and if another conquer himself, he is the greatest of conquerors. 104, 105. One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, not Mara with Brahman could change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself, and always lives under restraint.

items.name: Verse 106: If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a...

sections.Verse: 106. If a man for a hundred years sacrifice month after month with a thousand, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

items.name: Verse 107: If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest,...

sections.Verse: 107. If a man for a hundred years worship Agni (fire) in the forest, and if he but for one moment pay homage to a man whose soul is grounded (in true knowledge), better is that homage than sacrifice for a hundred years.

items.name: Verse 108: Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an...

sections.Verse: 108. Whatever a man sacrifice in this world as an offering or as an oblation for a whole year in order to gain merit, the whole of it is not worth a quarter (a farthing); reverence shown to the righteous is better.

items.name: Verse 109: He who always greets and constantly reveres the aged, four...

sections.Verse: 109. He who always greets and constantly reveres the aged, four things will increase to him, viz. life, beauty, happiness, power.

items.name: Verse 110: But he who lives a hundred years, vicious and unrestrained, a...

sections.Verse: 110. But he who lives a hundred years, vicious and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is virtuous and reflecting.

items.name: Verse 111: And he who lives a hundred years, ignorant and unrestrained, a...

sections.Verse: 111. And he who lives a hundred years, ignorant and unrestrained, a life of one day is better if a man is wise and reflecting.

items.name: Verse 112: And he who lives a hundred years, idle and weak, a life of one...

sections.Verse: 112. And he who lives a hundred years, idle and weak, a life of one day is better if a man has attained firm strength.

items.name: Verse 113: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing beginning and end,...

sections.Verse: 113. And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing beginning and end, a life of one day is better if a man sees beginning and end.

items.name: Verse 114: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the immortal place,...

sections.Verse: 114. And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the immortal place, a life of one day is better if a man sees the immortal place.

items.name: Verse 115: And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the highest law, a...

sections.Verse: 115. And he who lives a hundred years, not seeing the highest law, a life of one day is better if a man sees the highest law.

items.name: Chapter VIII: The Thousands

sections.Brief: Better than a thousand hollow words is one word that brings peace. Better than a thousand hollow verses is one verse that calms the mind. Victory over self is the greatest of all victories.

items.name: Verse 116: If a man would hasten towards the good, he should keep his...

sections.Verse: 116. If a man would hasten towards the good, he should keep his thought away from evil; if a man does what is good slothfully, his mind delights in evil.

items.name: Verse 117: If a man commits a sin, let him not do it again; let him not...

sections.Verse: 117. If a man commits a sin, let him not do it again; let him not delight in sin: pain is the outcome of evil.

items.name: Verse 118: If a man does what is good, let him do it again; let him delight...

sections.Verse: 118. If a man does what is good, let him do it again; let him delight in it: happiness is the outcome of good.

items.name: Verse 119: Even an evil-doer sees happiness as long as his evil deed has...

sections.Verse: 119. Even an evil-doer sees happiness as long as his evil deed has not ripened; but when his evil deed has ripened, then does the evil-doer see evil.

items.name: Verse 120: Even a good man sees evil days, as long as his good deed has not...

sections.Verse: 120. Even a good man sees evil days, as long as his good deed has not ripened; but when his good deed has ripened, then does the good man see happy days.

items.name: Verse 121: Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will...

sections.Verse: 121. Let no man think lightly of evil, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the fool becomes full of evil, even if he gather it little by little.

items.name: Verse 122: Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will...

sections.Verse: 122. Let no man think lightly of good, saying in his heart, It will not come nigh unto me. Even by the falling of water-drops a water-pot is filled; the wise man becomes full of good, even if he gather it little by little.

items.name: Verse 123: Let a man avoid evil deeds, as a merchant, if he has few...

sections.Verse: 123. Let a man avoid evil deeds, as a merchant, if he has few companions and carries much wealth, avoids a dangerous road; as a man who loves life avoids poison.

items.name: Verse 124: He who has no wound on his hand, may touch poison with his hand;...

sections.Verse: 124. He who has no wound on his hand, may touch poison with his hand; poison does not affect one who has no wound; nor is there evil for one who does not commit evil.

items.name: Verse 125: If a man offend a harmless, pure, and innocent person, the evil...

sections.Verse: 125. If a man offend a harmless, pure, and innocent person, the evil falls back upon that fool, like light dust thrown up against the wind.

items.name: Verse 126: Some people are born again; evil-doers go to hell; righteous...

sections.Verse: 126. Some people are born again; evil-doers go to hell; righteous people go to heaven; those who are free from all worldly desires attain Nirvana.

items.name: Verse 127: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter...

sections.Verse: 127. Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where a man might be freed from an evil deed.

items.name: Verse 128: Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter...

sections.Verse: 128. Not in the sky, not in the midst of the sea, not if we enter into the clefts of the mountains, is there known a spot in the whole world where death could not overcome (the mortal).

items.name: Chapter IX: Evil

sections.Brief: If a man commits sin, let him not do it again and again. The accumulation of evil brings suffering. Even an evil-doer sees good as long as the evil has not ripened; but when it has, then the evil-doer sees evil.

items.name: Verse 129: All men tremble at punishment, all men fear death; remember that...

sections.Verse: 129. All men tremble at punishment, all men fear death; remember that you are like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.

items.name: Verse 130: All men tremble at punishment, all men love life; remember that...

sections.Verse: 130. All men tremble at punishment, all men love life; remember that thou art like unto them, and do not kill, nor cause slaughter.

items.name: Verse 131: He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who...

sections.Verse: 131. He who seeking his own happiness punishes or kills beings who also long for happiness, will not find happiness after death.

items.name: Verse 132: He who seeking his own happiness does not punish or kill beings...

sections.Verse: 132. He who seeking his own happiness does not punish or kill beings who also long for happiness, will find happiness after death.

items.name: Verse 133: Do not speak harshly to anybody; those who are spoken to will...

sections.Verse: 133. Do not speak harshly to anybody; those who are spoken to will answer thee in the same way. Angry speech is painful, blows for blows will touch thee.

items.name: Verse 134: If, like a shattered metal plate (gong), thou utter not, then...

sections.Verse: 134. If, like a shattered metal plate (gong), thou utter not, then thou hast reached Nirvana; contention is not known to thee.

items.name: Verse 135: As a cowherd with his staff drives his cows into the stable, so...

sections.Verse: 135. As a cowherd with his staff drives his cows into the stable, so do Age and Death drive the life of men.

items.name: Verse 136: A fool does not know when he commits his evil deeds: but the...

sections.Verse: 136. A fool does not know when he commits his evil deeds: but the wicked man burns by his own deeds, as if burnt by fire.

items.name: Verse 137: He who inflicts pain on innocent and harmless persons, will soon...

sections.Verse: 137. He who inflicts pain on innocent and harmless persons, will soon come to one of these ten states:

items.name: Verse 138: He will have cruel suffering, loss, injury of the body, heavy...

sections.Verse: 138. He will have cruel suffering, loss, injury of the body, heavy affliction, or loss of mind,

items.name: Verse 139: Or a misfortune coming from the king, or a fearful accusation,...

sections.Verse: 139. Or a misfortune coming from the king, or a fearful accusation, or loss of relations, or destruction of treasures,

items.name: Verse 140: Or lightning-fire will burn his houses; and when his body is...

sections.Verse: 140. Or lightning-fire will burn his houses; and when his body is destroyed, the fool will go to hell.

items.name: Verse 141: Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying...

sections.Verse: 141. Not nakedness, not platted hair, not dirt, not fasting, or lying on the earth, not rubbing with dust, not sitting motionless, can purify a mortal who has not overcome desires.

items.name: Verse 142: He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exercises tranquillity,...

sections.Verse: 142. He who, though dressed in fine apparel, exercises tranquillity, is quiet, subdued, restrained, chaste, and has ceased to find fault with all other beings, he indeed is a Brahmana, an ascetic (sramana), a friar (bhikshu).

items.name: Verse 143: Is there in this world any man so restrained by humility that he...

sections.Verse: 143. Is there in this world any man so restrained by humility that he does not mind reproof, as a well-trained horse the whip?

items.name: Verse 144: Like a well-trained horse when touched by the whip, be ye active...

sections.Verse: 144. Like a well-trained horse when touched by the whip, be ye active and lively, and by faith, by virtue, by energy, by meditation, by discernment of the law you will overcome this great pain (of reproof), perfect in knowledge and in behaviour, and never forgetful.

items.name: Verse 145: Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend...

sections.Verse: 145. Well-makers lead the water (wherever they like); fletchers bend the arrow; carpenters bend a log of wood; good people fashion themselves.

items.name: Chapter X: Punishment

sections.Brief: All beings tremble before punishment and death. Remembering this, do not kill or cause to kill. He who seeking his own happiness injures others is not freed from hatred.

items.name: Verse 146: How is there laughter, how is there joy, as this world is always...

sections.Verse: 146. How is there laughter, how is there joy, as this world is always burning? Why do you not seek a light, ye who are surrounded by darkness?

items.name: Verse 147: Look at this dressed-up lump, covered with wounds, joined...

sections.Verse: 147. Look at this dressed-up lump, covered with wounds, joined together, sickly, full of many thoughts, which has no strength, no hold!

items.name: Verse 148: This body is wasted, full of sickness, and frail; this heap of...

sections.Verse: 148. This body is wasted, full of sickness, and frail; this heap of corruption breaks to pieces, life indeed ends in death.

items.name: Verse 149: Those white bones, like gourds thrown away in the autumn, what...

sections.Verse: 149. Those white bones, like gourds thrown away in the autumn, what pleasure is there in looking at them?

items.name: Verse 150: After a stronghold has been made of the bones, it is covered...

sections.Verse: 150. After a stronghold has been made of the bones, it is covered with flesh and blood, and there dwell in it old age and death, pride and deceit.

items.name: Verse 151: The brilliant chariots of kings are destroyed, the body also...

sections.Verse: 151. The brilliant chariots of kings are destroyed, the body also approaches destruction, but the virtue of good people never approaches destruction,--thus do the good say to the good.

items.name: Verse 152: A man who has learnt little, grows old like an ox; his flesh...

sections.Verse: 152. A man who has learnt little, grows old like an ox; his flesh grows, but his knowledge does not grow. 153, 154. Looking for the maker of this tabernacle, I shall have to run through a course of many births, so long as I do not find (him); and painful is birth again and again. But now, maker of the tabernacle, thou hast been seen; thou shalt not make up this tabernacle again. All thy rafters are broken, thy ridge-pole is sundered; the mind, approaching the Eternal (visankhara, nirvana), has attained to the extinction of all desires.

items.name: Verse 155: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained...

sections.Verse: 155. Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, perish like old herons in a lake without fish.

items.name: Verse 156: Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained...

sections.Verse: 156. Men who have not observed proper discipline, and have not gained treasure in their youth, lie, like broken bows, sighing after the past.

items.name: Chapter XI: Old Age

sections.Brief: What joy is there in this aging body, this nest of disease? Having seen this painted form, the wise are not attached. This body is worn out, a nest of diseases, perishable. Life ends in death.

items.name: Verse 157: If a man hold himself dear, let him watch himself carefully;...

sections.Verse: 157. If a man hold himself dear, let him watch himself carefully; during one at least out of the three watches a wise man should be watchful.

items.name: Verse 158: Let each man direct himself first to what is proper, then let...

sections.Verse: 158. Let each man direct himself first to what is proper, then let him teach others; thus a wise man will not suffer.

items.name: Verse 159: If a man make himself as he teaches others to be, then, being...

sections.Verse: 159. If a man make himself as he teaches others to be, then, being himself well subdued, he may subdue (others); one's own self is indeed difficult to subdue.

items.name: Verse 160: Self is the lord of self, who else could be the lord? With self...

sections.Verse: 160. Self is the lord of self, who else could be the lord? With self well subdued, a man finds a lord such as few can find.

items.name: Verse 161: The evil done by oneself, self-begotten, self-bred, crushes the...

sections.Verse: 161. The evil done by oneself, self-begotten, self-bred, crushes the foolish, as a diamond breaks a precious stone.

items.name: Verse 162: He whose wickedness is very great brings himself down to that...

sections.Verse: 162. He whose wickedness is very great brings himself down to that state where his enemy wishes him to be, as a creeper does with the tree which it surrounds.

items.name: Verse 163: Bad deeds, and deeds hurtful to ourselves, are easy to do; what...

sections.Verse: 163. Bad deeds, and deeds hurtful to ourselves, are easy to do; what is beneficial and good, that is very difficult to do.

items.name: Verse 164: The foolish man who scorns the rule of the venerable (Arahat),...

sections.Verse: 164. The foolish man who scorns the rule of the venerable (Arahat), of the elect (Ariya), of the virtuous, and follows false doctrine, he bears fruit to his own destruction, like the fruits of the Katthaka reed.

items.name: Verse 165: By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself...

sections.Verse: 165. By oneself the evil is done, by oneself one suffers; by oneself evil is left undone, by oneself one is purified. Purity and impurity belong to oneself, no one can purify another.

items.name: Verse 166: Let no one forget his own duty for the sake of another's,...

sections.Verse: 166. Let no one forget his own duty for the sake of another's, however great; let a man, after he has discerned his own duty, be always attentive to his duty.

items.name: Chapter XII: Self

sections.Brief: The self is the lord of the self — who else could be the lord? With self well subdued, a man finds a lord difficult to find. The evil done by oneself devours the fool.

items.name: Verse 167: Do not follow the evil law! Do not live on in thoughtlessness!...

sections.Verse: 167. Do not follow the evil law! Do not live on in thoughtlessness! Do not follow false doctrine! Be not a friend of the world.

items.name: Verse 168: Rouse thyself! do not be idle! Follow the law of virtue! The...

sections.Verse: 168. Rouse thyself! do not be idle! Follow the law of virtue! The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.

items.name: Verse 169: Follow the law of virtue; do not follow that of sin. The...

sections.Verse: 169. Follow the law of virtue; do not follow that of sin. The virtuous rests in bliss in this world and in the next.

items.name: Verse 170: Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage: the...

sections.Verse: 170. Look upon the world as a bubble, look upon it as a mirage: the king of death does not see him who thus looks down upon the world.

items.name: Verse 171: Come, look at this glittering world, like unto a royal chariot;...

sections.Verse: 171. Come, look at this glittering world, like unto a royal chariot; the foolish are immersed in it, but the wise do not touch it.

items.name: Verse 172: He who formerly was reckless and afterwards became sober,...

sections.Verse: 172. He who formerly was reckless and afterwards became sober, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.

items.name: Verse 173: He whose evil deeds are covered by good deeds, brightens up this...

sections.Verse: 173. He whose evil deeds are covered by good deeds, brightens up this world, like the moon when freed from clouds.

items.name: Verse 174: This world is dark, few only can see here; a few only go to...

sections.Verse: 174. This world is dark, few only can see here; a few only go to heaven, like birds escaped from the net.

items.name: Verse 175: The swans go on the path of the sun, they go through the ether...

sections.Verse: 175. The swans go on the path of the sun, they go through the ether by means of their miraculous power; the wise are led out of this world, when they have conquered Mara and his train.

items.name: Verse 176: If a man has transgressed one law, and speaks lies, and scoffs...

sections.Verse: 176. If a man has transgressed one law, and speaks lies, and scoffs at another world, there is no evil he will not do.

items.name: Verse 177: The uncharitable do not go to the world of the gods; fools only...

sections.Verse: 177. The uncharitable do not go to the world of the gods; fools only do not praise liberality; a wise man rejoices in liberality, and through it becomes blessed in the other world.

items.name: Verse 178: Better than sovereignty over the earth, better than going to...

sections.Verse: 178. Better than sovereignty over the earth, better than going to heaven, better than lordship over all worlds, is the reward of the first step in holiness.

items.name: Chapter XIII: The World

sections.Brief: Do not follow the way of the world but walk the path of righteousness. The world is like a bubble, like a mirage. The king of death does not see the one who regards the world thus.

items.name: Verse 179: He whose conquest is not conquered again, into whose conquest no...

sections.Verse: 179. He whose conquest is not conquered again, into whose conquest no one in this world enters, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?

items.name: Verse 180: He whom no desire with its snares and poisons can lead astray,...

sections.Verse: 180. He whom no desire with its snares and poisons can lead astray, by what track can you lead him, the Awakened, the Omniscient, the trackless?

items.name: Verse 181: Even the gods envy those who are awakened and not forgetful, who...

sections.Verse: 181. Even the gods envy those who are awakened and not forgetful, who are given to meditation, who are wise, and who delight in the repose of retirement (from the world).

items.name: Verse 182: Difficult (to obtain) is the conception of men, difficult is the...

sections.Verse: 182. Difficult (to obtain) is the conception of men, difficult is the life of mortals, difficult is the hearing of the True Law, difficult is the birth of the Awakened (the attainment of Buddhahood).

items.name: Verse 183: Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind,...

sections.Verse: 183. Not to commit any sin, to do good, and to purify one's mind, that is the teaching of (all) the Awakened.

items.name: Verse 184: The Awakened call patience the highest penance, long-suffering...

sections.Verse: 184. The Awakened call patience the highest penance, long-suffering the highest Nirvana; for he is not an anchorite (pravragita) who strikes others, he is not an ascetic (sramana) who insults others.

items.name: Verse 185: Not to blame, not to strike, to live restrained under the law,...

sections.Verse: 185. Not to blame, not to strike, to live restrained under the law, to be moderate in eating, to sleep and sit alone, and to dwell on the highest thoughts,--this is the teaching of the Awakened.

items.name: Verse 186: There is no satisfying lusts, even by a shower of gold pieces;...

sections.Verse: 186. There is no satisfying lusts, even by a shower of gold pieces; he who knows that lusts have a short taste and cause pain, he is wise;

items.name: Verse 187: Even in heavenly pleasures he finds no satisfaction, the...

sections.Verse: 187. Even in heavenly pleasures he finds no satisfaction, the disciple who is fully awakened delights only in the destruction of all desires.

items.name: Verse 188: Men, driven by fear, go to many a refuge, to mountains and...

sections.Verse: 188. Men, driven by fear, go to many a refuge, to mountains and forests, to groves and sacred trees.

items.name: Verse 189: But that is not a safe refuge, that is not the best refuge; a...

sections.Verse: 189. But that is not a safe refuge, that is not the best refuge; a man is not delivered from all pains after having gone to that refuge.

items.name: Verse 190: He who takes refuge with Buddha, the Law, and the Church; he...

sections.Verse: 190. He who takes refuge with Buddha, the Law, and the Church; he who, with clear understanding, sees the four holy truths:--

items.name: Verse 191: Viz. pain, the origin of pain, the destruction of pain, and the...

sections.Verse: 191. Viz. pain, the origin of pain, the destruction of pain, and the eightfold holy way that leads to the quieting of pain;--

items.name: Verse 192: That is the safe refuge, that is the best refuge; having gone to...

sections.Verse: 192. That is the safe refuge, that is the best refuge; having gone to that refuge, a man is delivered from all pain.

items.name: Verse 193: A supernatural person (a Buddha) is not easily found, he is not...

sections.Verse: 193. A supernatural person (a Buddha) is not easily found, he is not born everywhere. Wherever such a sage is born, that race prospers.

items.name: Verse 194: Happy is the arising of the awakened, happy is the teaching of...

sections.Verse: 194. Happy is the arising of the awakened, happy is the teaching of the True Law, happy is peace in the church, happy is the devotion of those who are at peace. 195, 196. He who pays homage to those who deserve homage, whether the awakened (Buddha) or their disciples, those who have overcome the host (of evils), and crossed the flood of sorrow, he who pays homage to such as have found deliverance and know no fear, his merit can never be measured by anybody.

items.name: Chapter XIV: The Buddha (The Awakened)

sections.Brief: The Buddha is awakened, his path is difficult to understand. Victorious and free from craving, he teaches by silence and example. Even the gods envy the awakened ones.

items.name: Verse 197: Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! among...

sections.Verse: 197. Let us live happily then, not hating those who hate us! among men who hate us let us dwell free from hatred!

items.name: Verse 198: Let us live happily then, free from ailments among the ailing!...

sections.Verse: 198. Let us live happily then, free from ailments among the ailing! among men who are ailing let us dwell free from ailments!

items.name: Verse 199: Let us live happily then, free from greed among the greedy!...

sections.Verse: 199. Let us live happily then, free from greed among the greedy! among men who are greedy let us dwell free from greed!

items.name: Verse 200: Let us live happily then, though we call nothing our own! We...

sections.Verse: 200. Let us live happily then, though we call nothing our own! We shall be like the bright gods, feeding on happiness!

items.name: Verse 201: Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has...

sections.Verse: 201. Victory breeds hatred, for the conquered is unhappy. He who has given up both victory and defeat, he, the contented, is happy.

items.name: Verse 202: There is no fire like passion; there is no losing throw like...

sections.Verse: 202. There is no fire like passion; there is no losing throw like hatred; there is no pain like this body; there is no happiness higher than rest.

items.name: Verse 203: Hunger is the worst of diseases, the body the greatest of pains;...

sections.Verse: 203. Hunger is the worst of diseases, the body the greatest of pains; if one knows this truly, that is Nirvana, the highest happiness.

items.name: Verse 204: Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches;...

sections.Verse: 204. Health is the greatest of gifts, contentedness the best riches; trust is the best of relationships, Nirvana the highest happiness.

items.name: Verse 205: He who has tasted the sweetness of solitude and tranquillity, is...

sections.Verse: 205. He who has tasted the sweetness of solitude and tranquillity, is free from fear and free from sin, while he tastes the sweetness of drinking in the law.

items.name: Verse 206: The sight of the elect (Arya) is good, to live with them is...

sections.Verse: 206. The sight of the elect (Arya) is good, to live with them is always happiness; if a man does not see fools, he will be truly happy.

items.name: Verse 207: He who walks in the company of fools suffers a long way; company...

sections.Verse: 207. He who walks in the company of fools suffers a long way; company with fools, as with an enemy, is always painful; company with the wise is pleasure, like meeting with kinsfolk.

items.name: Verse 208: Therefore, one ought to follow the wise, the intelligent, the...

sections.Verse: 208. Therefore, one ought to follow the wise, the intelligent, the learned, the much enduring, the dutiful, the elect; one ought to follow a good and wise man, as the moon follows the path of the stars.

items.name: Chapter XV: Happiness

sections.Brief: Happy is the arising of the awakened. Happy is the teaching of the True Law. Happy is the harmony of the Sangha. Let us live happily among the hateful, among the sick, among the greedy — free from greed.

items.name: Verse 209: He who gives himself to vanity, and does not give himself to...

sections.Verse: 209. He who gives himself to vanity, and does not give himself to meditation, forgetting the real aim (of life) and grasping at pleasure, will in time envy him who has exerted himself in meditation.

items.name: Verse 210: Let no man ever look for what is pleasant, or what is...

sections.Verse: 210. Let no man ever look for what is pleasant, or what is unpleasant. Not to see what is pleasant is pain, and it is pain to see what is unpleasant.

items.name: Verse 211: Let, therefore, no man love anything; loss of the beloved is...

sections.Verse: 211. Let, therefore, no man love anything; loss of the beloved is evil. Those who love nothing and hate nothing, have no fetters.

items.name: Verse 212: From pleasure comes grief, from pleasure comes fear; he who is...

sections.Verse: 212. From pleasure comes grief, from pleasure comes fear; he who is free from pleasure knows neither grief nor fear.

items.name: Verse 213: From affection comes grief, from affection comes fear; he who is...

sections.Verse: 213. From affection comes grief, from affection comes fear; he who is free from affection knows neither grief nor fear.

items.name: Verse 214: From lust comes grief, from lust comes fear; he who is free from...

sections.Verse: 214. From lust comes grief, from lust comes fear; he who is free from lust knows neither grief nor fear.

items.name: Verse 215: From love comes grief, from love comes fear; he who is free from...

sections.Verse: 215. From love comes grief, from love comes fear; he who is free from love knows neither grief nor fear.

items.name: Verse 216: From greed comes grief, from greed comes fear; he who is free...

sections.Verse: 216. From greed comes grief, from greed comes fear; he who is free from greed knows neither grief nor fear.

items.name: Verse 217: He who possesses virtue and intelligence, who is just, speaks...

sections.Verse: 217. He who possesses virtue and intelligence, who is just, speaks the truth, and does what is his own business, him the world will hold dear.

items.name: Verse 218: He in whom a desire for the Ineffable (Nirvana) has sprung up,...

sections.Verse: 218. He in whom a desire for the Ineffable (Nirvana) has sprung up, who is satisfied in his mind, and whose thoughts are not bewildered by love, he is called urdhvamsrotas (carried upwards by the stream).

items.name: Verse 219: Kinsmen, friends, and lovers salute a man who has been long...

sections.Verse: 219. Kinsmen, friends, and lovers salute a man who has been long away, and returns safe from afar.

items.name: Verse 220: In like manner his good works receive him who has done good, and...

sections.Verse: 220. In like manner his good works receive him who has done good, and has gone from this world to the other;--as kinsmen receive a friend on his return.

items.name: Chapter XVI: Pleasure

sections.Brief: He who gives himself to vanity and does not give himself to meditation is like one who gathers no fruit in the forest. Do not pursue pleasure, for the thoughtful attain a happiness greater than any pleasure.

items.name: Verse 221: Let a man leave anger, let him forsake pride, let him overcome...

sections.Verse: 221. Let a man leave anger, let him forsake pride, let him overcome all bondage! No sufferings befall the man who is not attached to name and form, and who calls nothing his own.

items.name: Verse 222: He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot, him I...

sections.Verse: 222. He who holds back rising anger like a rolling chariot, him I call a real driver; other people are but holding the reins.

items.name: Verse 223: Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good;...

sections.Verse: 223. Let a man overcome anger by love, let him overcome evil by good; let him overcome the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth!

items.name: Verse 224: Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked...

sections.Verse: 224. Speak the truth, do not yield to anger; give, if thou art asked for little; by these three steps thou wilt go near the gods.

items.name: Verse 225: The sages who injure nobody, and who always control their body,...

sections.Verse: 225. The sages who injure nobody, and who always control their body, they will go to the unchangeable place (Nirvana), where, if they have gone, they will suffer no more.

items.name: Verse 226: Those who are ever watchful, who study day and night, and who...

sections.Verse: 226. Those who are ever watchful, who study day and night, and who strive after Nirvana, their passions will come to an end.

items.name: Verse 227: This is an old saying, O Atula, this is not only of to-day:...

sections.Verse: 227. This is an old saying, O Atula, this is not only of to-day: `They blame him who sits silent, they blame him who speaks much, they also blame him who says little; there is no one on earth who is not blamed.'

items.name: Verse 228: There never was, there never will be, nor is there now, a man...

sections.Verse: 228. There never was, there never will be, nor is there now, a man who is always blamed, or a man who is always praised. 229, 230. But he whom those who discriminate praise continually day after day, as without blemish, wise, rich in knowledge and virtue, who would dare to blame him, like a coin made of gold from the Gambu river? Even the gods praise him, he is praised even by Brahman.

items.name: Verse 231: Beware of bodily anger, and control thy body! Leave the sins of...

sections.Verse: 231. Beware of bodily anger, and control thy body! Leave the sins of the body, and with thy body practise virtue!

items.name: Verse 232: Beware of the anger of the tongue, and control thy tongue! Leave...

sections.Verse: 232. Beware of the anger of the tongue, and control thy tongue! Leave the sins of the tongue, and practise virtue with thy tongue!

items.name: Verse 233: Beware of the anger of the mind, and control thy mind! Leave the...

sections.Verse: 233. Beware of the anger of the mind, and control thy mind! Leave the sins of the mind, and practise virtue with thy mind!

items.name: Verse 234: The wise who control their body, who control their tongue, the...

sections.Verse: 234. The wise who control their body, who control their tongue, the wise who control their mind, are indeed well controlled.

items.name: Chapter XVII: Anger

sections.Brief: Let a man overcome anger by love, evil by good, the greedy by liberality, the liar by truth. Speak the truth, do not yield to anger. The wise who control body, speech, and mind are truly well-controlled.

items.name: Verse 235: Thou art now like a sear leaf, the messengers of death (Yama)...

sections.Verse: 235. Thou art now like a sear leaf, the messengers of death (Yama) have come near to thee; thou standest at the door of thy departure, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.

items.name: Verse 236: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities...

sections.Verse: 236. Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt enter into the heavenly world of the elect (Ariya).

items.name: Verse 237: Thy life has come to an end, thou art come near to death (Yama),...

sections.Verse: 237. Thy life has come to an end, thou art come near to death (Yama), there is no resting-place for thee on the road, and thou hast no provision for thy journey.

items.name: Verse 238: Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities...

sections.Verse: 238. Make thyself an island, work hard, be wise! When thy impurities are blown away, and thou art free from guilt, thou wilt not enter again into birth and decay.

items.name: Verse 239: Let a wise man blow off the impurities of his self, as a smith...

sections.Verse: 239. Let a wise man blow off the impurities of his self, as a smith blows off the impurities of silver one by one, little by little, and from time to time.

items.name: Verse 240: As the impurity which springs from the iron, when it springs...

sections.Verse: 240. As the impurity which springs from the iron, when it springs from it, destroys it; thus do a transgressor's own works lead him to the evil path.

items.name: Verse 241: The taint of prayers is non-repetition; the taint of houses,...

sections.Verse: 241. The taint of prayers is non-repetition; the taint of houses, non-repair; the taint of the body is sloth; the taint of a watchman, thoughtlessness.

items.name: Verse 242: Bad conduct is the taint of woman, greediness the taint of a...

sections.Verse: 242. Bad conduct is the taint of woman, greediness the taint of a benefactor; tainted are all evil ways in this world and in the next.

items.name: Verse 243: But there is a taint worse than all taints,--ignorance is the...

sections.Verse: 243. But there is a taint worse than all taints,--ignorance is the greatest taint. O mendicants! throw off that taint, and become taintless!

items.name: Verse 244: Life is easy to live for a man who is without shame, a crow...

sections.Verse: 244. Life is easy to live for a man who is without shame, a crow hero, a mischief-maker, an insulting, bold, and wretched fellow.

items.name: Verse 245: But life is hard to live for a modest man, who always looks for...

sections.Verse: 245. But life is hard to live for a modest man, who always looks for what is pure, who is disinterested, quiet, spotless, and intelligent.

items.name: Verse 246: He who destroys life, who speaks untruth, who in this world...

sections.Verse: 246. He who destroys life, who speaks untruth, who in this world takes what is not given him, who goes to another man's wife;

items.name: Verse 247: And the man who gives himself to drinking intoxicating liquors,...

sections.Verse: 247. And the man who gives himself to drinking intoxicating liquors, he, even in this world, digs up his own root.

items.name: Verse 248: O man, know this, that the unrestrained are in a bad state; take...

sections.Verse: 248. O man, know this, that the unrestrained are in a bad state; take care that greediness and vice do not bring thee to grief for a long time!

items.name: Verse 249: The world gives according to their faith or according to their...

sections.Verse: 249. The world gives according to their faith or according to their pleasure: if a man frets about the food and the drink given to others, he will find no rest either by day or by night.

items.name: Verse 250: He in whom that feeling is destroyed, and taken out with the...

sections.Verse: 250. He in whom that feeling is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, finds rest by day and by night.

items.name: Verse 251: There is no fire like passion, there is no shark like hatred,...

sections.Verse: 251. There is no fire like passion, there is no shark like hatred, there is no snare like folly, there is no torrent like greed.

items.name: Verse 252: The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is...

sections.Verse: 252. The fault of others is easily perceived, but that of oneself is difficult to perceive; a man winnows his neighbour's faults like chaff, but his own fault he hides, as a cheat hides the bad die from the gambler.

items.name: Verse 253: If a man looks after the faults of others, and is always...

sections.Verse: 253. If a man looks after the faults of others, and is always inclined to be offended, his own passions will grow, and he is far from the destruction of passions.

items.name: Verse 254: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by...

sections.Verse: 254. There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. The world delights in vanity, the Tathagatas (the Buddhas) are free from vanity.

items.name: Verse 255: There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by...

sections.Verse: 255. There is no path through the air, a man is not a Samana by outward acts. No creatures are eternal; but the awakened (Buddha) are never shaken.

items.name: Chapter XVIII: Impurity

sections.Brief: The impure are revealed by their actions. Do not look at the faults of others but at your own deeds. Like a beautiful flower that is fragrant, the well-spoken word bears fruit in one who practices it.

items.name: Verse 258: A man is not learned because he talks much; he who is patient,...

sections.Verse: 258. A man is not learned because he talks much; he who is patient, free from hatred and fear, he is called learned.

items.name: Verse 259: A man is not a supporter of the law because he talks much; even...

sections.Verse: 259. A man is not a supporter of the law because he talks much; even if a man has learnt little, but sees the law bodily, he is a supporter of the law, a man who never neglects the law.

items.name: Verse 260: A man is not an elder because his head is grey; his age may be...

sections.Verse: 260. A man is not an elder because his head is grey; his age may be ripe, but he is called `Old-in-vain.'

items.name: Verse 261: He in whom there is truth, virtue, love, restraint, moderation,...

sections.Verse: 261. He in whom there is truth, virtue, love, restraint, moderation, he who is free from impurity and is wise, he is called an elder.

items.name: Verse 262: An envious greedy, dishonest man does not become respectable by...

sections.Verse: 262. An envious greedy, dishonest man does not become respectable by means of much talking only, or by the beauty of his complexion.

items.name: Verse 263: He in whom all this is destroyed, and taken out with the very...

sections.Verse: 263. He in whom all this is destroyed, and taken out with the very root, he, when freed from hatred and wise, is called respectable.

items.name: Verse 264: Not by tonsure does an undisciplined man who speaks falsehood...

sections.Verse: 264. Not by tonsure does an undisciplined man who speaks falsehood become a Samana; can a man be a Samana who is still held captive by desire and greediness?

items.name: Verse 265: He who always quiets the evil, whether small or large, he is...

sections.Verse: 265. He who always quiets the evil, whether small or large, he is called a Samana (a quiet man), because he has quieted all evil.

items.name: Verse 266: A man is not a mendicant (Bhikshu) simply because he asks others...

sections.Verse: 266. A man is not a mendicant (Bhikshu) simply because he asks others for alms; he who adopts the whole law is a Bhikshu, not he who only begs.

items.name: Verse 267: He who is above good and evil, who is chaste, who with knowledge...

sections.Verse: 267. He who is above good and evil, who is chaste, who with knowledge passes through the world, he indeed is called a Bhikshu. 268, 269. A man is not a Muni because he observes silence (mona, i.e. mauna), if he is foolish and ignorant; but the wise who, taking the balance, chooses the good and avoids evil, he is a Muni, and is a Muni thereby; he who in this world weighs both sides is called a Muni.

items.name: Verse 270: A man is not an elect (Ariya) because he injures living...

sections.Verse: 270. A man is not an elect (Ariya) because he injures living creatures; because he has pity on all living creatures, therefore is a man called Ariya. 271, 272. Not only by discipline and vows, not only by much learning, not by entering into a trance, not by sleeping alone, do I earn the happiness of release which no worldling can know. Bhikshu, be not confident as long as thou hast not attained the extinction of desires.

items.name: Chapter XIX: The Just

sections.Brief: He is not just who decides matters arbitrarily. The wise who investigate both right and wrong, who judge others with fairness and impartiality — they are guardians of the law, the just.

items.name: Verse 273: The best of ways is the eightfold; the best of truths the four...

sections.Verse: 273. The best of ways is the eightfold; the best of truths the four words; the best of virtues passionlessness; the best of men he who has eyes to see.

items.name: Verse 274: This is the way, there is no other that leads to the purifying...

sections.Verse: 274. This is the way, there is no other that leads to the purifying of intelligence. Go on this way! Everything else is the deceit of Mara (the tempter).

items.name: Verse 275: If you go on this way, you will make an end of pain! The way was...

sections.Verse: 275. If you go on this way, you will make an end of pain! The way was preached by me, when I had understood the removal of the thorns (in the flesh).

items.name: Verse 276: You yourself must make an effort. The Tathagatas (Buddhas) are...

sections.Verse: 276. You yourself must make an effort. The Tathagatas (Buddhas) are only preachers. The thoughtful who enter the way are freed from the bondage of Mara.

items.name: Verse 277: `All created things perish,' he who knows and sees this becomes...

sections.Verse: 277. `All created things perish,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way to purity.

items.name: Verse 278: `All created things are grief and pain,' he who knows and sees...

sections.Verse: 278. `All created things are grief and pain,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.

items.name: Verse 279: `All forms are unreal,' he who knows and sees this becomes...

sections.Verse: 279. `All forms are unreal,' he who knows and sees this becomes passive in pain; this is the way that leads to purity.

items.name: Verse 280: He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who,...

sections.Verse: 280. He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who, though young and strong, is full of sloth, whose will and thought are weak, that lazy and idle man will never find the way to knowledge.

items.name: Verse 281: Watching his speech, well restrained in mind, let a man never...

sections.Verse: 281. Watching his speech, well restrained in mind, let a man never commit any wrong with his body! Let a man but keep these three roads of action clear, and he will achieve the way which is taught by the wise.

items.name: Verse 282: Through zeal knowledge is gotten, through lack of zeal knowledge...

sections.Verse: 282. Through zeal knowledge is gotten, through lack of zeal knowledge is lost; let a man who knows this double path of gain and loss thus place himself that knowledge may grow.

items.name: Verse 283: Cut down the whole forest (of lust), not a tree only! Danger...

sections.Verse: 283. Cut down the whole forest (of lust), not a tree only! Danger comes out of the forest (of lust). When you have cut down both the forest (of lust) and its undergrowth, then, Bhikshus, you will be rid of the forest and free!

items.name: Verse 284: So long as the love of man towards women, even the smallest, is...

sections.Verse: 284. So long as the love of man towards women, even the smallest, is not destroyed, so long is his mind in bondage, as the calf that drinks milk is to its mother.

items.name: Verse 285: Cut out the love of self, like an autumn lotus, with thy hand!...

sections.Verse: 285. Cut out the love of self, like an autumn lotus, with thy hand! Cherish the road of peace. Nirvana has been shown by Sugata (Buddha).

items.name: Verse 286: `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,'...

sections.Verse: 286. `Here I shall dwell in the rain, here in winter and summer,' thus the fool meditates, and does not think of his death.

items.name: Verse 287: Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children...

sections.Verse: 287. Death comes and carries off that man, praised for his children and flocks, his mind distracted, as a flood carries off a sleeping village.

items.name: Verse 288: Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help...

sections.Verse: 288. Sons are no help, nor a father, nor relations; there is no help from kinsfolk for one whom death has seized.

items.name: Verse 289: A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should...

sections.Verse: 289. A wise and good man who knows the meaning of this, should quickly clear the way that leads to Nirvana.

items.name: Chapter XX: The Way

sections.Brief: The best of paths is the Eightfold Path. The best of truths are the Four Noble Truths. Freedom from attachment is the best state. The best of men is the seeing one.

items.name: Verse 290: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a...

sections.Verse: 290. If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure, and look to the great.

items.name: Verse 291: He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for...

sections.Verse: 291. He who, by causing pain to others, wishes to obtain pleasure for himself, he, entangled in the bonds of hatred, will never be free from hatred.

items.name: Verse 292: What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is...

sections.Verse: 292. What ought to be done is neglected, what ought not to be done is done; the desires of unruly, thoughtless people are always increasing.

items.name: Verse 293: But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their...

sections.Verse: 293. But they whose whole watchfulness is always directed to their body, who do not follow what ought not to be done, and who steadfastly do what ought to be done, the desires of such watchful and wise people will come to an end.

items.name: Verse 294: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father...

sections.Verse: 294. A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two valiant kings, though he has destroyed a kingdom with all its subjects.

items.name: Verse 295: A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father...

sections.Verse: 295. A true Brahmana goes scatheless, though he have killed father and mother, and two holy kings, and an eminent man besides.

items.name: Verse 296: The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and...

sections.Verse: 296. The disciples of Gotama (Buddha) are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on Buddha.

items.name: Verse 297: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their...

sections.Verse: 297. The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the law.

items.name: Verse 298: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their...

sections.Verse: 298. The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on the church.

items.name: Verse 299: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their...

sections.Verse: 299. The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their thoughts day and night are always set on their body.

items.name: Verse 300: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind...

sections.Verse: 300. The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in compassion.

items.name: Verse 301: The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind...

sections.Verse: 301. The disciples of Gotama are always well awake, and their mind day and night always delights in meditation.

items.name: Verse 302: It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to...
