sections.Verse: 302. It is hard to leave the world (to become a friar), it is hard to enjoy the world; hard is the monastery, painful are the houses; painful it is to dwell with equals (to share everything in common) and the itinerant mendicant is beset with pain. Therefore let no man be an itinerant mendicant and he will not be beset with pain.

items.name: Verse 303: Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man...

sections.Verse: 303. Whatever place a faithful, virtuous, celebrated, and wealthy man chooses, there he is respected.

items.name: Verse 304: Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad...

sections.Verse: 304. Good people shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; bad people are not seen, like arrows shot by night.

items.name: Verse 305: He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting...

sections.Verse: 305. He alone who, without ceasing, practises the duty of sitting alone and sleeping alone, he, subduing himself, will rejoice in the destruction of all desires alone, as if living in a forest.

items.name: Chapter XXI: Miscellaneous

sections.Brief: If by leaving a small pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let a wise man leave the small pleasure and look to the great. Cattle are led to slaughter — so are the days of mortals.

items.name: Verse 306: He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done...

sections.Verse: 306. He who says what is not, goes to hell; he also who, having done a thing, says I have not done it. After death both are equal, they are men with evil deeds in the next world.

items.name: Verse 307: Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are...

sections.Verse: 307. Many men whose shoulders are covered with the yellow gown are ill-conditioned and unrestrained; such evil-doers by their evil deeds go to hell.

items.name: Verse 308: Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring...

sections.Verse: 308. Better it would be to swallow a heated iron ball, like flaring fire, than that a bad unrestrained fellow should live on the charity of the land.

items.name: Verse 309: Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's...

sections.Verse: 309. Four things does a wreckless man gain who covets his neighbour's wife,--a bad reputation, an uncomfortable bed, thirdly, punishment, and lastly, hell.

items.name: Verse 310: There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is...

sections.Verse: 310. There is bad reputation, and the evil way (to hell), there is the short pleasure of the frightened in the arms of the frightened, and the king imposes heavy punishment; therefore let no man think of his neighbour's wife.

items.name: Verse 311: As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm,...

sections.Verse: 311. As a grass-blade, if badly grasped, cuts the arm, badly-practised asceticism leads to hell.

items.name: Verse 312: An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating...

sections.Verse: 312. An act carelessly performed, a broken vow, and hesitating obedience to discipline, all this brings no great reward.

items.name: Verse 313: If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it...

sections.Verse: 313. If anything is to be done, let a man do it, let him attack it vigorously! A careless pilgrim only scatters the dust of his passions more widely.

items.name: Verse 314: An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it...

sections.Verse: 314. An evil deed is better left undone, for a man repents of it afterwards; a good deed is better done, for having done it, one does not repent.

items.name: Verse 315: Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and...

sections.Verse: 315. Like a well-guarded frontier fort, with defences within and without, so let a man guard himself. Not a moment should escape, for they who allow the right moment to pass, suffer pain when they are in hell.

items.name: Verse 316: They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of,...

sections.Verse: 316. They who are ashamed of what they ought not to be ashamed of, and are not ashamed of what they ought to be ashamed of, such men, embracing false doctrines enter the evil path.

items.name: Verse 317: They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when...

sections.Verse: 317. They who fear when they ought not to fear, and fear not when they ought to fear, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

items.name: Verse 318: They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and...

sections.Verse: 318. They who forbid when there is nothing to be forbidden, and forbid not when there is something to be forbidden, such men, embracing false doctrines, enter the evil path.

items.name: Verse 319: They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not...

sections.Verse: 319. They who know what is forbidden as forbidden, and what is not forbidden as not forbidden, such men, embracing the true doctrine, enter the good path.

items.name: Chapter XXII: The Downward Course

sections.Brief: The person who utters false speech goes to hell. Those who, having done evil, say 'I did not do it' also go to a downward course. Both go to the same state after death — humans of wicked deeds in the next world.

items.name: Verse 320: Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures...

sections.Verse: 320. Silently shall I endure abuse as the elephant in battle endures the arrow sent from the bow: for the world is ill-natured.

items.name: Verse 321: They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed...

sections.Verse: 321. They lead a tamed elephant to battle, the king mounts a tamed elephant; the tamed is the best among men, he who silently endures abuse.

items.name: Verse 322: Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants...

sections.Verse: 322. Mules are good, if tamed, and noble Sindhu horses, and elephants with large tusks; but he who tames himself is better still.

items.name: Verse 323: For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country...

sections.Verse: 323. For with these animals does no man reach the untrodden country (Nirvana), where a tamed man goes on a tamed animal, viz. on his own well-tamed self.

items.name: Verse 324: The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap,...

sections.Verse: 324. The elephant called Dhanapalaka, his temples running with sap, and difficult to hold, does not eat a morsel when bound; the elephant longs for the elephant grove.

items.name: Verse 325: If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and...

sections.Verse: 325. If a man becomes fat and a great eater, if he is sleepy and rolls himself about, that fool, like a hog fed on wash, is born again and again.

items.name: Verse 326: This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as...

sections.Verse: 326. This mind of mine went formerly wandering about as it liked, as it listed, as it pleased; but I shall now hold it in thoroughly, as the rider who holds the hook holds in the furious elephant.

items.name: Verse 327: Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of...

sections.Verse: 327. Be not thoughtless, watch your thoughts! Draw yourself out of the evil way, like an elephant sunk in mud.

items.name: Verse 328: If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise,...

sections.Verse: 328. If a man find a prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, he may walk with him, overcoming all dangers, happy, but considerate.

items.name: Verse 329: If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise,...

sections.Verse: 329. If a man find no prudent companion who walks with him, is wise, and lives soberly, let him walk alone, like a king who has left his conquered country behind,--like an elephant in the forest.

items.name: Verse 330: It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a...

sections.Verse: 330. It is better to live alone, there is no companionship with a fool; let a man walk alone, let him commit no sin, with few wishes, like an elephant in the forest.

items.name: Verse 331: If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is...

sections.Verse: 331. If an occasion arises, friends are pleasant; enjoyment is pleasant, whatever be the cause; a good work is pleasant in the hour of death; the giving up of all grief is pleasant.

items.name: Verse 332: Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the...

sections.Verse: 332. Pleasant in the world is the state of a mother, pleasant the state of a father, pleasant the state of a Samana, pleasant the state of a Brahmana.

items.name: Verse 333: Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith...

sections.Verse: 333. Pleasant is virtue lasting to old age, pleasant is a faith firmly rooted; pleasant is attainment of intelligence, pleasant is avoiding of sins.

items.name: Chapter XXIII: The Elephant

sections.Brief: If on the road you meet no one your equal or better, travel alone. Companionship with a fool is unworthy. Like a royal elephant in battle enduring arrows, the wise endure abuse with patience.

items.name: Verse 334: The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs...

sections.Verse: 334. The thirst of a thoughtless man grows like a creeper; he runs from life to life, like a monkey seeking fruit in the forest.

items.name: Verse 335: Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this...

sections.Verse: 335. Whomsoever this fierce thirst overcomes, full of poison, in this world, his sufferings increase like the abounding Birana grass.

items.name: Verse 336: He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered...

sections.Verse: 336. He who overcomes this fierce thirst, difficult to be conquered in this world, sufferings fall off from him, like water-drops from a lotus leaf.

items.name: Verse 337: This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here...

sections.Verse: 337. This salutary word I tell you, `Do ye, as many as are here assembled, dig up the root of thirst, as he who wants the sweet-scented Usira root must dig up the Birana grass, that Mara (the tempter) may not crush you again and again, as the stream crushes the reeds.'

items.name: Verse 338: As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as...

sections.Verse: 338. As a tree, even though it has been cut down, is firm so long as its root is safe, and grows again, thus, unless the feeders of thirst are destroyed, the pain (of life) will return again and again.

items.name: Verse 339: He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in...

sections.Verse: 339. He whose thirst running towards pleasure is exceeding strong in the thirty-six channels, the waves will carry away that misguided man, viz. his desires which are set on passion.

items.name: Verse 340: The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands...

sections.Verse: 340. The channels run everywhere, the creeper (of passion) stands sprouting; if you see the creeper springing up, cut its root by means of knowledge.

items.name: Verse 341: A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in...

sections.Verse: 341. A creature's pleasures are extravagant and luxurious; sunk in lust and looking for pleasure, men undergo (again and again) birth and decay.

items.name: Verse 342: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in...

sections.Verse: 342. Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; held in fetters and bonds, they undergo pain for a long time, again and again.

items.name: Verse 343: Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let...

sections.Verse: 343. Men, driven on by thirst, run about like a snared hare; let therefore the mendicant drive out thirst, by striving after passionlessness for himself.

items.name: Verse 344: He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having...

sections.Verse: 344. He who having got rid of the forest (of lust) (i.e. after having reached Nirvana) gives himself over to forest-life (i.e. to lust), and who, when removed from the forest (i.e. from lust), runs to the forest (i.e. to lust), look at that man! though free, he runs into bondage.

items.name: Verse 345: Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of...

sections.Verse: 345. Wise people do not call that a strong fetter which is made of iron, wood, or hemp; far stronger is the care for precious stones and rings, for sons and a wife.

items.name: Verse 346: That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields,...

sections.Verse: 346. That fetter wise people call strong which drags down, yields, but is difficult to undo; after having cut this at last, people leave the world, free from cares, and leaving desires and pleasures behind.

items.name: Verse 347: Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of...

sections.Verse: 347. Those who are slaves to passions, run down with the stream (of desires), as a spider runs down the web which he has made himself; when they have cut this, at last, wise people leave the world free from cares, leaving all affection behind.

items.name: Verse 348: Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is...

sections.Verse: 348. Give up what is before, give up what is behind, give up what is in the middle, when thou goest to the other shore of existence; if thy mind is altogether free, thou wilt not again enter into birth and decay.

items.name: Verse 349: If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and...

sections.Verse: 349. If a man is tossed about by doubts, full of strong passions, and yearning only for what is delightful, his thirst will grow more and more, and he will indeed make his fetters strong.

items.name: Verse 350: If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting,...

sections.Verse: 350. If a man delights in quieting doubts, and, always reflecting, dwells on what is not delightful (the impurity of the body, &c.), he certainly will remove, nay, he will cut the fetter of Mara.

items.name: Verse 351: He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who...

sections.Verse: 351. He who has reached the consummation, who does not tremble, who is without thirst and without sin, he has broken all the thorns of life: this will be his last body.

items.name: Verse 352: He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands...

sections.Verse: 352. He who is without thirst and without affection, who understands the words and their interpretation, who knows the order of letters (those which are before and which are after), he has received his last body, he is called the great sage, the great man.

items.name: Verse 353: `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I...

sections.Verse: 353. `I have conquered all, I know all, in all conditions of life I am free from taint; I have left all, and through the destruction of thirst I am free; having learnt myself, whom shall I teach?'

items.name: Verse 354: The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law...

sections.Verse: 354. The gift of the law exceeds all gifts; the sweetness of the law exceeds all sweetness; the delight in the law exceeds all delights; the extinction of thirst overcomes all pain.

items.name: Verse 355: Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other...

sections.Verse: 355. Pleasures destroy the foolish, if they look not for the other shore; the foolish by his thirst for pleasures destroys himself, as if he were his own enemy.

items.name: Verse 356: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion:...

sections.Verse: 356. The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by passion: therefore a gift bestowed on the passionless brings great reward.

items.name: Verse 357: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred:...

sections.Verse: 357. The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by hatred: therefore a gift bestowed on those who do not hate brings great reward.

items.name: Verse 358: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity:...

sections.Verse: 358. The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by vanity: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from vanity brings great reward.

items.name: Verse 359: The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by lust:...

sections.Verse: 359. The fields are damaged by weeds, mankind is damaged by lust: therefore a gift bestowed on those who are free from lust brings great reward.

items.name: Chapter XXIV: Thirst

sections.Brief: Thirst (tanha/craving) is the cause of suffering. The streams of craving flow everywhere. Cut them with wisdom. He who has destroyed craving crosses the flood that is so difficult to cross.

items.name: Verse 360: Restraint in the eye is good, good is restraint in the ear, in...

sections.Verse: 360. Restraint in the eye is good, good is restraint in the ear, in the nose restraint is good, good is restraint in the tongue.

items.name: Verse 361: In the body restraint is good, good is restraint in speech, in...

sections.Verse: 361. In the body restraint is good, good is restraint in speech, in thought restraint is good, good is restraint in all things. A Bhikshu, restrained in all things, is freed from all pain.

items.name: Verse 362: He who controls his hand, he who controls his feet, he who...

sections.Verse: 362. He who controls his hand, he who controls his feet, he who controls his speech, he who is well controlled, he who delights inwardly, who is collected, who is solitary and content, him they call Bhikshu.

items.name: Verse 363: The Bhikshu who controls his mouth, who speaks wisely and...

sections.Verse: 363. The Bhikshu who controls his mouth, who speaks wisely and calmly, who teaches the meaning and the law, his word is sweet.

items.name: Verse 364: He who dwells in the law, delights in the law, meditates on the...

sections.Verse: 364. He who dwells in the law, delights in the law, meditates on the law, follows the law, that Bhikshu will never fall away from the true law.

items.name: Verse 365: Let him not despise what he has received, nor ever envy others:...

sections.Verse: 365. Let him not despise what he has received, nor ever envy others: a mendicant who envies others does not obtain peace of mind.

items.name: Verse 366: A Bhikshu who, though he receives little, does not despise what...

sections.Verse: 366. A Bhikshu who, though he receives little, does not despise what he has received, even the gods will praise him, if his life is pure, and if he is not slothful.

items.name: Verse 367: He who never identifies himself with name and form, and does not...

sections.Verse: 367. He who never identifies himself with name and form, and does not grieve over what is no more, he indeed is called a Bhikshu.

items.name: Verse 368: The Bhikshu who acts with kindness, who is calm in the doctrine...

sections.Verse: 368. The Bhikshu who acts with kindness, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha, will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.

items.name: Verse 369: O Bhikshu, empty this boat! if emptied, it will go quickly;...

sections.Verse: 369. O Bhikshu, empty this boat! if emptied, it will go quickly; having cut off passion and hatred thou wilt go to Nirvana.

items.name: Verse 370: Cut off the five (senses), leave the five, rise above the five....

sections.Verse: 370. Cut off the five (senses), leave the five, rise above the five. A Bhikshu, who has escaped from the five fetters, he is called Oghatinna, `saved from the flood.'

items.name: Verse 371: Meditate, O Bhikshu, and be not heedless! Do not direct thy...

sections.Verse: 371. Meditate, O Bhikshu, and be not heedless! Do not direct thy thought to what gives pleasure that thou mayest not for thy heedlessness have to swallow the iron ball (in hell), and that thou mayest not cry out when burning, `This is pain.'

items.name: Verse 372: Without knowledge there is no meditation, without meditation...

sections.Verse: 372. Without knowledge there is no meditation, without meditation there is no knowledge: he who has knowledge and meditation is near unto Nirvana.

items.name: Verse 373: A Bhikshu who has entered his empty house, and whose mind is...

sections.Verse: 373. A Bhikshu who has entered his empty house, and whose mind is tranquil, feels a more than human delight when he sees the law clearly.

items.name: Verse 374: As soon as he has considered the origin and destruction of the...

sections.Verse: 374. As soon as he has considered the origin and destruction of the elements (khandha) of the body, he finds happiness and joy which belong to those who know the immortal (Nirvana).

items.name: Verse 375: And this is the beginning here for a wise Bhikshu: watchfulness...

sections.Verse: 375. And this is the beginning here for a wise Bhikshu: watchfulness over the senses, contentedness, restraint under the law; keep noble friends whose life is pure, and who are not slothful.

items.name: Verse 376: Let him live in charity, let him be perfect in his duties; then...

sections.Verse: 376. Let him live in charity, let him be perfect in his duties; then in the fulness of delight he will make an end of suffering.

items.name: Verse 377: As the Vassika plant sheds its withered flowers, men should shed...

sections.Verse: 377. As the Vassika plant sheds its withered flowers, men should shed passion and hatred, O ye Bhikshus!

items.name: Verse 378: The Bhikshu whose body and tongue and mind are quieted, who is...

sections.Verse: 378. The Bhikshu whose body and tongue and mind are quieted, who is collected, and has rejected the baits of the world, he is called quiet.

items.name: Verse 379: Rouse thyself by thyself, examine thyself by thyself, thus...

sections.Verse: 379. Rouse thyself by thyself, examine thyself by thyself, thus self-protected and attentive wilt thou live happily, O Bhikshu!

items.name: Verse 380: For self is the lord of self, self is the refuge of self;...

sections.Verse: 380. For self is the lord of self, self is the refuge of self; therefore curb thyself as the merchant curbs a good horse.

items.name: Verse 381: The Bhikshu, full of delight, who is calm in the doctrine of...

sections.Verse: 381. The Bhikshu, full of delight, who is calm in the doctrine of Buddha will reach the quiet place (Nirvana), cessation of natural desires, and happiness.

items.name: Verse 382: He who, even as a young Bhikshu, applies himself to the doctrine...

sections.Verse: 382. He who, even as a young Bhikshu, applies himself to the doctrine of Buddha, brightens up this world, like the moon when free from clouds.

items.name: Chapter XXV: The Bhikshu (Mendicant)

sections.Brief: The Bhikshu (mendicant monk) restrains his senses, is content, and walks the path of virtue. He is a true monk not by begging but by living the whole law, free from good and evil, walking with care in this world.

items.name: Verse 383: Stop the stream valiantly, drive away the desires, O Brahmana!...

sections.Verse: 383. Stop the stream valiantly, drive away the desires, O Brahmana! When you have understood the destruction of all that was made, you will understand that which was not made.

items.name: Verse 384: If the Brahmana has reached the other shore in both laws (in...

sections.Verse: 384. If the Brahmana has reached the other shore in both laws (in restraint and contemplation), all bonds vanish from him who has obtained knowledge.

items.name: Verse 385: He for whom there is neither this nor that shore, nor both, him,...

sections.Verse: 385. He for whom there is neither this nor that shore, nor both, him, the fearless and unshackled, I call indeed a Brahmana.

items.name: Verse 386: He who is thoughtful, blameless, settled, dutiful, without...

sections.Verse: 386. He who is thoughtful, blameless, settled, dutiful, without passions, and who has attained the highest end, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

items.name: Verse 387: The sun is bright by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior...

sections.Verse: 387. The sun is bright by day, the moon shines by night, the warrior is bright in his armour, the Brahmana is bright in his meditation; but Buddha, the Awakened, is bright with splendour day and night.

items.name: Verse 388: Because a man is rid of evil, therefore he is called Brahmana;...

sections.Verse: 388. Because a man is rid of evil, therefore he is called Brahmana; because he walks quietly, therefore he is called Samana; because he has sent away his own impurities, therefore he is called Pravragita (Pabbagita, a pilgrim).

items.name: Verse 389: No one should attack a Brahmana, but no Brahmana (if attacked)...

sections.Verse: 389. No one should attack a Brahmana, but no Brahmana (if attacked) should let himself fly at his aggressor! Woe to him who strikes a Brahmana, more woe to him who flies at his aggressor!

items.name: Verse 390: It advantages a Brahmana not a little if he holds his mind back...

sections.Verse: 390. It advantages a Brahmana not a little if he holds his mind back from the pleasures of life; when all wish to injure has vanished, pain will cease.

items.name: Verse 391: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not offend by body, word,...

sections.Verse: 391. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not offend by body, word, or thought, and is controlled on these three points.

items.name: Verse 392: After a man has once understood the law as taught by the...

sections.Verse: 392. After a man has once understood the law as taught by the Well-awakened (Buddha), let him worship it carefully, as the Brahmana worships the sacrificial fire.

items.name: Verse 393: A man does not become a Brahmana by his platted hair, by his...

sections.Verse: 393. A man does not become a Brahmana by his platted hair, by his family, or by birth; in whom there is truth and righteousness, he is blessed, he is a Brahmana.

items.name: Verse 394: What is the use of platted hair, O fool! what of the raiment of...

sections.Verse: 394. What is the use of platted hair, O fool! what of the raiment of goat-skins? Within thee there is ravening, but the outside thou makest clean.

items.name: Verse 395: The man who wears dirty raiments, who is emaciated and covered...

sections.Verse: 395. The man who wears dirty raiments, who is emaciated and covered with veins, who lives alone in the forest, and meditates, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

items.name: Verse 396: I do not call a man a Brahmana because of his origin or of his...

sections.Verse: 396. I do not call a man a Brahmana because of his origin or of his mother. He is indeed arrogant, and he is wealthy: but the poor, who is free from all attachments, him I call indeed a Brahmana.

items.name: Verse 397: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut all fetters, who never...

sections.Verse: 397. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut all fetters, who never trembles, is independent and unshackled.

items.name: Verse 398: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut the strap and the...

sections.Verse: 398. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has cut the strap and the thong, the chain with all that pertains to it, who has burst the bar, and is awakened.

items.name: Verse 399: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, though he has committed no...

sections.Verse: 399. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, though he has committed no offence, endures reproach, bonds, and stripes, who has endurance for his force, and strength for his army.

items.name: Verse 400: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is free from anger, dutiful,...

sections.Verse: 400. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is free from anger, dutiful, virtuous, without appetite, who is subdued, and has received his last body.

items.name: Verse 401: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not cling to pleasures,...

sections.Verse: 401. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who does not cling to pleasures, like water on a lotus leaf, like a mustard seed on the point of a needle.

items.name: Verse 402: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, even here, knows the end of...

sections.Verse: 402. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, even here, knows the end of his suffering, has put down his burden, and is unshackled.

items.name: Verse 403: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose knowledge is deep, who...

sections.Verse: 403. Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose knowledge is deep, who possesses wisdom, who knows the right way and the wrong, and has attained the highest end.

items.name: Verse 404: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who keeps aloof both from laymen...

sections.Verse: 404. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who keeps aloof both from laymen and from mendicants, who frequents no houses, and has but few desires.

items.name: Verse 405: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who finds no fault with other...

sections.Verse: 405. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who finds no fault with other beings, whether feeble or strong, and does not kill nor cause slaughter.

items.name: Verse 406: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is tolerant with the...

sections.Verse: 406. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is tolerant with the intolerant, mild with fault-finders, and free from passion among the passionate.

items.name: Verse 407: Him I call indeed a Brahmana from whom anger and hatred, pride...

sections.Verse: 407. Him I call indeed a Brahmana from whom anger and hatred, pride and envy have dropt like a mustard seed from the point of a needle.

items.name: Verse 408: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who utters true speech, instructive...

sections.Verse: 408. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who utters true speech, instructive and free from harshness, so that he offend no one.

items.name: Verse 409: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who takes nothing in the world that...

sections.Verse: 409. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who takes nothing in the world that is not given him, be it long or short, small or large, good or bad.

items.name: Verse 410: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who fosters no desires for this...

sections.Verse: 410. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who fosters no desires for this world or for the next, has no inclinations, and is unshackled.

items.name: Verse 411: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has no interests, and when he...

sections.Verse: 411. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has no interests, and when he has understood (the truth), does not say How, how? and who has reached the depth of the Immortal.

items.name: Verse 412: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world is above good and...

sections.Verse: 412. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world is above good and evil, above the bondage of both, free from grief from sin, and from impurity.

items.name: Verse 413: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is bright like the moon, pure,...

sections.Verse: 413. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who is bright like the moon, pure, serene, undisturbed, and in whom all gaiety is extinct.

items.name: Verse 414: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has traversed this miry road,...

sections.Verse: 414. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has traversed this miry road, the impassable world and its vanity, who has gone through, and reached the other shore, is thoughtful, guileless, free from doubts, free from attachment, and content.

items.name: Verse 415: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world, leaving all...

sections.Verse: 415. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who in this world, leaving all desires, travels about without a home, and in whom all concupiscence is extinct.

items.name: Verse 416: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, leaving all longings, travels...

sections.Verse: 416. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, leaving all longings, travels about without a home, and in whom all covetousness is extinct.

items.name: Verse 417: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, after leaving all bondage to...

sections.Verse: 417. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who, after leaving all bondage to men, has risen above all bondage to the gods, and is free from all and every bondage.

items.name: Verse 418: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has left what gives pleasure...

sections.Verse: 418. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who has left what gives pleasure and what gives pain, who is cold, and free from all germs (of renewed life), the hero who has conquered all the worlds.

items.name: Verse 419: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows the destruction and the...

sections.Verse: 419. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows the destruction and the return of beings everywhere, who is free from bondage, welfaring (Sugata), and awakened (Buddha).

items.name: Verse 420: Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose path the gods do not know,...

sections.Verse: 420. Him I call indeed a Brahmana whose path the gods do not know, nor spirits (Gandharvas), nor men, whose passions are extinct, and who is an Arhat (venerable).

items.name: Verse 421: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who calls nothing his own, whether...

sections.Verse: 421. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who calls nothing his own, whether it be before, behind, or between, who is poor, and free from the love of the world.

items.name: Verse 422: Him I call indeed a Brahmana, the manly, the noble, the hero,...

sections.Verse: 422. Him I call indeed a Brahmana, the manly, the noble, the hero, the great sage, the conqueror, the impassible, the accomplished, the awakened.

items.name: Verse 423: Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows his former abodes, who...

sections.Verse: 423. Him I call indeed a Brahmana who knows his former abodes, who sees heaven and hell, has reached the end of births, is perfect in knowledge, a sage, and whose perfections are all perfect.

items.name: Chapter XXVI: The Brahmana (Arhat)

sections.Brief: The true Brahmana is not one by birth but by conduct. He has crossed beyond attachment, is free from anger and desire, and has reached the shore beyond. He whom no pleasure moves, who has completed the journey — him I call a Brahmana.
